clue: An interpreted, high-level, general-purpose programming language named after a snake.
A classic word-guessing game.
A logic-based, combinatorial number-placement puzzle.
A finite set of unambiguous instructions that, given some input, can produce a result.
A computer program that translates computer code written in one programming language into another.
An organized collection of data, generally stored and accessed electronically.
A computer program or device that provides a service to another computer program and its user.
A group of two or more computer systems other devices linked together.
The process of converting information or data into a code, especially to prevent unauthorized access.
A storage location paired with an associated symbolic name, which contains some value.
A block of organized, reusable code that is used to perform a single, related action.
Relating to or involving a program or routine of which a part of the instructions are repeated.
An object that enables a programmer to traverse a container, particularly lists.
An associative array or mapping of unique keys to values in Python.
A binary variable that can have one of two states: True or False.
A datum of integral data type, a number that can be written without a fractional component.
A sequence of characters, either as a literal constant or as some kind of variable.
A programming language object that stores the memory address of another value.
The process of finding and resolving bugs within computer program code.
A collection of instructions and data that tells a computer how to work.
The physical parts of a computer, such as the case, central processing unit, monitor, etc.
The minimum amount of any physical entity involved in an interaction.
A natural phenomenon by which all things with mass or energy are brought toward one another.
The natural science that studies matter, its fundamental constituents, its motion and behavior.
The natural science that studies life and living organisms, including their physical structure.
The scientific study of the properties and behavior of matter.
A systematic enterprise that builds and organizes knowledge in the form of testable explanations.
A group of two or more atoms held together by chemical bonds.
A subatomic particle whose electric charge is negative one elementary charge.
A subatomic particle, symbol n, with no net electric charge and a mass slightly larger than a proton.
A subatomic particle, symbol p, with a positive electric charge of +1e.
The very dense region consisting of protons and neutrons at the center of an atom.
A gravitationally bound system of stars, stellar remnants, interstellar gas, dust, and dark matter.
An interstellar cloud of dust, hydrogen, helium and other ionized gases.
A minor planet of the inner Solar System.
An icy, small Solar System body that, when passing close to the Sun, warms and begins to release gases.
An astronomical body orbiting a star or stellar remnant that is massive enough to be rounded by gravity.
An artificial object which has been intentionally placed into orbit in outer space.
An optical instrument using lenses, curved mirrors, or a combination of both to observe distant objects.
A laboratory instrument used to examine objects that are too small to be seen by the naked eye.
The directional speed of an object in motion as an indication of its rate of change in position.
The product of the mass and velocity of an object.
The force resisting the relative motion of solid surfaces, fluid layers, and material elements sliding.
The resistance of any physical object to any change in its velocity.
The quantitative property that must be transferred to a body or physical system to perform work.
A scientific concept, as well as a measurable physical property, commonly associated with disorder.
The emission or transmission of energy in the form of waves or particles through space or a medium.
A nuclear reaction or a radioactive decay process in which the nucleus of an atom splits into smaller parts.
A reaction in which two or more atomic nuclei are combined to form one or more different atomic nuclei.
A rupture in the crust of a planetary-mass object, such as Earth, that allows hot lava to escape.
The shaking of the surface of the Earth resulting from a sudden release of energy in the lithosphere.
A rapidly rotating storm system characterized by a low-pressure center, a strong wind spiral.
A series of waves in a water body caused by the displacement of a large volume of water.
A persistent body of dense ice that is constantly moving under its own weight.
A community of living organisms in conjunction with the nonliving components of their environment.
The worldwide sum of all ecosystems, also known as the zone of life on Earth.
A layer or a set of layers of gases surrounding a planet or other material body.
A chemical element with the symbol N and atomic number 7, a common diatomic gas.
A highly reactive nonmetal and oxidizing agent that readily forms oxides with most elements.
The chemical element with the symbol H and atomic number 1, lightest element.
A colorless, odorless, tasteless, non-toxic, inert, monatomic gas, first in the noble gases.
A chemical element with the symbol C and atomic number 6, tetravalent nonmetal.
A hard, brittle crystalline solid with a blue-grey metallic luster, a tetravalent metalloid.
A chemical element with the symbol Ca and atomic number 20, essential alkaline earth metal.
A soft, silvery-white, highly reactive alkali metal, element number 11.
A silvery-white metal that is soft enough to be cut with a knife with little effort, element 19.
A shiny gray alkaline earth metal, element number 12, highly flammable in air when thin.
A yellow-green halogen gas, element number 17, highly reactive and strong oxidizing agent.
Relating to computers, information technology, and virtual reality.
A global system of interconnected computer networks that uses the Internet protocol suite.
A collection of web pages and related content that is identified by a common domain name.
A software application for accessing information on the World Wide Web.
Small blocks of data created by a web server while a user is browsing a website.
A system of rules that explain how data is sent between computers.
A network security system that monitors and controls incoming and outgoing network traffic.
Any software intentionally designed to cause disruption to a computer, server, client, or computer network.
A type of social engineering attack often used to steal user data, including login credentials.
An information technology expert who uses technical knowledge to achieve a goal or overcome an obstacle.
A word or string of characters used for user authentication to prove identity or access approval.
The protection of computer systems and networks from information disclosure, theft, or damage.
A numeric system that only uses two digits — 0 and 1.
A positional numeral system with a radix, or base, of 16.
The base-8 number system, uses digits 0 to 7.
A rectangular array or table of numbers, symbols, or expressions, arranged in rows and columns.
A geometric object that has magnitude and direction, or a 1D sequence in code.
A physical quantity that can be described by a single number, unlike vectors.
An algebraic object that describes a multilinear relationship between sets of algebraic objects.
The mathematical study of continuous change, in the same way that geometry is the study of shape.
One of the broad areas of mathematics, together with number theory, geometry and analysis.
A branch of mathematics concerned with properties of space such as distance, shape, size.
The mathematical study of the properties that are preserved through deformations, twistings.
The discipline that concerns the collection, organization, analysis, interpretation, and presentation of data.
The branch of mathematics concerning numerical descriptions of how likely an event is to occur.
The average of a set of numbers, calculated by dividing the sum of the values by their count.
The value separating the higher half from the lower half of a data sample.
The value that appears most frequently in a data set.
A measure of dispersion, meaning it is a measure of how far a set of numbers are spread out.
A measure of difference between the observed value of a variable and some other value.
The rate of change of a function with respect to a variable in calculus.
A mathematical object that can be interpreted as an area or a generalization of area in calculus.
A concept describing something without any bound, or something larger than any natural number.
A mathematical operation, written as b to the power of n, involving base and power.
A numerical quantity that is not a whole number, represented as a numerator over a denominator.
A system of numbers based on the number ten, tenths, hundredths.
A number or ratio expressed as a fraction of 100.
A relationship between two numbers indicating how many times the first number contains the second.
A statement that two ratios are equal, or a comparative relation between things.
A mathematical statement that asserts the equality of two expressions.
A relation which makes a non-equal comparison between two numbers or mathematical expressions.
A value that does not change during the execution of a program or in mathematics.
A statement that has been proven, or can be proven, on the basis of explicitly accepted formulas.
